summary: I was born and raised in Somalia, and it was my home until the civil war started in 1991. The war spread throughout the entire country, making it unsafe for families like mine. I lost my mother during the early days of the conflict, which left a deep hole in my heart. Eventually, I had to leave Somalia behind to find safety and opportunity elsewhere.

Mogadishu is where I grew up, the capital city of Somalia. It was my home until the civil war began in 1991, and the violence made the city unsafe like the rest of the country. Leaving Mogadishu was difficult because it was where all my family and memories were. I left the city in 2004 to come to the United States, hoping to build a better life.

The Somali Civil War started in 1991 and changed everything for me and my family. It spread across the country, making no place safe, and tragically, my mother was one of the many who lost their lives during that time. The war forced me to leave Somalia and eventually come to the United States in 2004. It was a painful chapter, but it also shaped my determination to create a better future.

I came to the United States in 2004 when I was 21 years old, joining my husband who lived here. The U.S. became my new home and a place of opportunity, even though I left behind six siblings in Somalia. I worked hard, starting with housekeeping jobs, while attending night school to learn English and later earn my high school diploma. The United States gave me the chance to pursue education and build a future for my children and myself.
